cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The process by which sensory receptors detect and convert physical energy into neural signals

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The brain's process of organizing and interpreting sensory information to give it meaning

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The conversion of physical energy into neural impulses the brain can interpret

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The minimum stimulation needed to detect a stimulus 50% of the time

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The minimum difference between two stimuli required for detection 50% of the time

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The principle that the JND is a constant proportion of the original stimulus

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A theory predicting when we detect faint signals amid noise, considering sensitivity and response bias

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Diminished sensitivity to a constant, unchanging stimulus over time

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Stimuli below the conscious threshold of awareness

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The transparent structure behind the pupil that focuses light onto the retina by changing shape

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The light-sensitive inner surface of the eye containing rods and cones

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Rods
cards.answer: Retinal receptors that detect dim light and peripheral vision; not sensitive to color

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Cones
cards.answer: Retinal receptors concentrated in the fovea that detect color and fine detail in bright light

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The central focal point of the retina where cones are concentrated for sharp, detailed vision

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The point where the optic nerve exits the eye, creating a gap in the visual field with no receptor cells

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The theory that color vision depends on three types of cones sensitive to red, green, or blue light

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The theory that color perception involves opposing neural processes for red-green, blue-yellow, and black-white

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Phenomenon explained by opponent-process theory: staring at a red image then seeing green on a white surface

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Inability to distinguish certain colors, usually red and green, due to missing or defective cone types

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The adjustable opening in the iris that controls the amount of light entering the eye

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The characteristic of sound determined by the amplitude (height) of the sound wave

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The characteristic of sound determined by the frequency (wavelength) of the sound wave

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The snail-shaped, fluid-filled structure in the inner ear where sound is transduced into neural signals

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The theory that different sound frequencies cause different locations on the basilar membrane to vibrate

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The theory that the rate of auditory nerve impulses matches the frequency of the incoming sound

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Hearing loss caused by damage to the cochlea's hair cells or the auditory nerve

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Hearing loss caused by damage to the mechanical system that conducts sound to the cochlea

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The sense of body position and movement provided by receptors in muscles, tendons, and joints

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The sense of balance and head position provided by the semicircular canals and vestibular sacs

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The theory that the spinal cord has a neural gate that blocks or allows pain signals to pass to the brain

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Information processing driven by higher-level knowledge, expectations, and context rather than raw data

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Information processing that starts with raw sensory data and builds up to pattern recognition

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A mental predisposition to perceive stimuli in a certain way based on expectations and experience

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Gestalt principle: objects near each other are perceived as belonging together

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Gestalt principle: similar objects are perceived as belonging together

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Gestalt principle: the brain fills in gaps to perceive incomplete figures as whole

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Gestalt principle: we perceive smooth, continuous patterns rather than disconnected elements

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Gestalt principle: we organize visual field into a prominent object (figure) and its surroundings (ground)

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Depth cue: each eye receives a slightly different image due to their horizontal separation

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Depth cue: the inward turning of eyes when focusing on a nearby object

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Monocular depth cue: closer objects partially block the view of objects behind them

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Monocular depth cue: distant objects appear smaller than nearby objects of the same size

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Monocular depth cue: parallel lines appear to converge as they recede into the distance

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The ability to perceive objects as having constant size, shape, and color despite changes in the retinal image

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A door appears rectangular whether it is open or closed — example of what type of constancy?

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Misleading visual stimuli that cause the brain to perceive something differently from physical reality

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Ernst Weber
cards.answer: Discovered that the just noticeable difference is proportional to the original stimulus (Weber's Law)

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Gustav Fechner
cards.answer: Founded psychophysics — the study of the relationship between physical stimuli and psychological experience

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The sense of smell, which uses chemoreceptors in the nasal cavity

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The sense of taste, which detects sweet, salty, sour, bitter, and umami through receptors on the tongue

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The three tiny bones in the middle ear that amplify sound vibrations from the eardrum to the cochlea

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Monocular depth cue: objects higher in the visual field appear farther away

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The focusing of light on the retina by changing the curvature of the lens

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The colored part of the eye that controls pupil size to regulate the amount of light entering

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: You stop noticing the smell of your house after being inside for a while — example of what process?
